does: This paper examines how AI safety and control systems safety can be unified to address human-AI interactions as dynamic feedback loops rather than isolated prediction problems. The authors argue that current AI safety approaches—typically focused on fine-tuning outputs to match human feedback—fail to account for how AI outputs influence human behavior over time, which in turn shapes subsequent AI inputs. They introduce a formal framework for modeling these safety-critical feedback loops between human users and AI systems, moving beyond static alignment paradigms to capture the temporal, interactive nature of real-world AI deployment where human responses to AI behavior fundamentally reshape the interaction dynamics.
connects: This work bridges two previously separate research traditions: generative AI safety, which has emphasized output alignment through techniques like RLHF and instruction following, and classical control systems safety, which has long studied feedback loops and stability in physical systems. The paper builds on decades of control theory research into closed-loop stability and robustness, while also engaging with recent AI safety literature on alignment, interpretability, and the challenges of specification gaming and reward hacking. It responds to growing recognition in the AI safety community that standard supervised learning approaches may be insufficient when deployed systems interact with humans who adaptively respond to AI behavior, drawing on insights from human-computer interaction and behavioral economics about feedback effects.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward safety verification methods that account for human-in-the-loop dynamics, potentially enabling development of AI systems with formal safety guarantees in interactive settings. The work could facilitate new approaches to adversarial robustness that consider not just evasion attacks but behavioral adaptation by human users. It may inspire hybrid research combining control-theoretic stability analysis with deep learning, development of safer deployment strategies that anticipate user responses, and novel evaluation benchmarks for human-AI systems that capture feedback loop effects. The formalism could also support more rigorous analysis of unintended consequences in deployed systems, where alignment failures manifest not as single bad outputs but as harmful equilibria emerging from repeated human-AI interaction.
